title: Resident room minimum square footage
content: Resident rooms. Bedrooms must measure at least 80 square feet per resident in multiple resident bedrooms, and at least 100 square feet in single resident rooms.

title: Resident rooms must have direct access to an exit corridor
content: Resident rooms. Bedrooms must have direct access to an exit corridor.

title: Resident rooms must have a window to the outside
content: Resident rooms. Bedrooms must have at least one window to the outside.

title: Resident room floor at or above grade
content: Resident rooms. Bedrooms must have a floor at or above grade level.

title: Each resident must have a separate bed of proper size and height
content: The facility must provide each resident with a separate bed of proper size and height for the safety and convenience of the resident.

title: Clean, comfortable mattress required
content: The facility must provide each resident with a clean, comfortable mattress.

title: Functional furniture and individual closet space
content: The facility must provide each resident with functional furniture appropriate to the resident's needs, and individual closet space in the resident's bedroom with clothes racks and shelves accessible to the resident.

title: Bathroom facilities equipped with or near each resident room
content: Bathroom facilities. Each resident room must be equipped with or located near toilet and bathing facilities.

title: Resident call system from bedside and toilet/bathing facilities
content: Resident call system. The facility must be adequately equipped to allow residents to call for staff assistance through a communication system which relays the call directly to a staff member or to a centralized staff work area from (1) each resident's bedside; and (2) toilet and bathing facilities.

title: Dining and resident activities rooms
content: Dining and resident activities. The facility must provide one or more rooms designated for resident dining and activities. These rooms must be well-lighted; well-ventilated; adequately furnished; and have sufficient space to accommodate all activities.

title: Safe, functional, sanitary and comfortable environment
content: Other environmental conditions. The facility must provide a safe, functional, sanitary, and comfortable environment for residents, staff and the public. The facility must, among other things, establish procedures to ensure water availability during a loss of normal water supply, provide adequate ventilation, install corridor handrails, maintain an effective pest control program, and establish policies regarding smoking.

source: CMS State Operations Manual Appendix PP (interpretive guidance)
citation: CMS SOM Appendix PP, F-tag F921 (guidance to 42 CFR §483.90(i))
title: Safe and comfortable temperature levels (71-81 F)
content: CMS interpretive guidance for the requirement to maintain safe and comfortable temperature levels: facilities initially certified after October 1, 1990 must maintain a temperature range of 71 to 81 degrees Fahrenheit. This is interpretive surveyor guidance operationalizing 42 CFR §483.90(i); the regulation text itself states the environment must be safe, functional, sanitary, and comfortable.

source: CMS State Operations Manual Appendix PP
citation: 42 CFR §483.25(d) / F-tag F689 (Free of Accident Hazards)
title: Free of accident hazards / fall prevention
content: The resident environment must remain as free of accident hazards as is possible, and each resident must receive adequate supervision and assistance devices to prevent accidents. Equipment that mitigates fall and entrapment risk - low beds, floor mats, properly fitted bed rails, grab bars, and slip-resistant flooring - supports compliance with the facility's obligation to prevent avoidable accidents.

source: FDA Hospital Bed System Dimensional Guidance (2006) + ASTM F1858
citation: FDA Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff: Hospital Bed System Dimensions and Assessment of Entrapment Risk (March 2006)
title: Hospital bed entrapment zone dimensional limits
content: FDA dimensional guidance to reduce hospital bed entrapment defines seven entrapment zones. Key limits: openings in the rail and between rail and mattress should be small enough to prevent head/neck entrapment - generally any gap that could permit a 4.75 inch (120 mm) diameter cone (head) or smaller dimensions for the neck should be avoided. Beds and rails procured for at-risk residents should document conformance with this guidance and ASTM F1858 hospital bed standards.

source: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code (2012 ed.), Ch.18 New Health Care Occupancies
title: Minimum exit access corridor clear width
content: Reference summary of NFPA 101 (2012) for new health care occupancies: aisles, corridors, and ramps required for exit access in a nursing home shall be at least 8 ft (2440 mm) in clear and unobstructed width, with limited exceptions. CMS adopts the 2012 Life Safety Code via 42 CFR §483.90(a). (Paraphrased reference; consult the licensed NFPA text for exact wording.)

source: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code (2012 ed.), Ch.18 New Health Care Occupancies
title: Minimum clear width of resident room egress doors
content: Reference summary of NFPA 101 (2012) for new health care occupancies: doors in the means of egress from sleeping rooms designed for the movement of beds shall provide a clear width of not less than 41.5 in (1055 mm). CMS adopts the 2012 Life Safety Code via 42 CFR §483.90(a). (Paraphrased reference; consult the licensed NFPA text for exact wording.)

source: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code (2012 ed.), Ch.18 New Health Care Occupancies
title: Smoke compartment size and resident limits
content: Reference summary of NFPA 101 (2012) for new health care occupancies: every story used by residents for sleeping or treatment shall be divided into smoke compartments not exceeding 22,500 sq ft, with travel distance to a smoke barrier door not exceeding 200 ft, and generally not more than 30 residents per smoke compartment. CMS adopts the 2012 Life Safety Code via 42 CFR §483.90(a). (Paraphrased reference; consult the licensed NFPA text for exact wording.)

source: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code (2012 ed.), Ch.18 New Health Care Occupancies
title: Automatic sprinkler protection required
content: Reference summary of NFPA 101 (2012) for new health care occupancies: buildings shall be protected throughout by an approved, supervised automatic sprinkler system installed in accordance with NFPA 13. CMS requires long-term care facilities to be fully sprinklered. (Paraphrased reference; consult the licensed NFPA text for exact wording.)

source: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code (2012 ed.), Ch.18 New Health Care Occupancies
title: Corridor doors and positive latching
content: Reference summary of NFPA 101 (2012) for new health care occupancies: doors protecting corridor openings shall resist the passage of smoke and be provided with positive latching hardware; roller latches are prohibited. (Paraphrased reference; consult the licensed NFPA text for exact wording.)

citation: 2010 ADA Standards §604 / §609 (grab bars at water closets)
title: Grab bar placement at toilets
content: Reference summary of the 2010 ADA Standards: accessible water closets require a side-wall and rear-wall grab bar; grab bars at water closets must be installed 33 in to 36 in above the floor. Accessible toileting and bathing fixtures support the facility's obligation under 42 CFR §483.90(f). (Paraphrased reference.)

title: Emergency electrical power source
content: Reference summary of 42 CFR §483.90(c): an emergency electrical power system must supply power adequate at least for lighting of exits, fire detection/alarm/extinguishing systems, and equipment needed to maintain life support and safe operation during interruption of normal power. (Paraphrased reference.)

source: CMS State Operations Manual Appendix PP
title: Cleanable, non-porous surfaces for infection control
content: Reference summary of 42 CFR §483.80 / F880: the facility must establish and maintain an infection prevention and control program. Furnishings, mattresses, and flooring procured for resident areas should present cleanable, non-porous, disinfectable surfaces to support effective environmental cleaning. (Paraphrased reference.)
